questions.question: Assume that the last element of the set is used as partition element in Quicksort. If n distinct elements from the set {1, 2, ..., n} are to be sorted, give an input for which Quicksort takes maximum time.
questions.answer: An already sorted sequence, e.g., 1, 2, 3, ..., n (ascending order) or n, n-1, ..., 2, 1 (descending order).
questions.to_find: An input arrangement of n distinct elements that causes Quicksort (last-element pivot) to take maximum (worst-case) time.

understand.plain: Quicksort picks the last element as the pivot and splits the array into two parts. Worst case happens when every pivot split is maximally unbalanced — one subarray has 0 elements and the other has n-1 elements. This occurs when the array is already sorted (ascending or descending), because the last element is always the largest (or smallest), leaving one partition empty.

keywords.explain: The element chosen to partition the array into two halves in Quicksort.
An input where every pivot selection creates a maximally unbalanced partition (sizes 0 and n-1).
keywords.example: Sorted array [1,2,3,4,5] with last element as pivot: pivot=5 leaves [1,2,3,4] | [].

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='120' viewBox='0 0 420 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='70' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><rect x='130' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='190' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><rect x='250' y='10' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><text x='280' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>pivot</text><text x='215' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sorted array: last element is always max => empty right partition => O(n^2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array [1,2,3,4,5] with element 5 (last) highlighted as pivot. Since 5 is the largest, partition gives [1,2,3,4] | [], causing maximally unbalanced splits at every level.

given.aim: Find an input of n distinct elements from {1,...,n} that maximizes the running time of last-element-pivot Quicksort.

terms.meaning: The rightmost element of the current subarray is always chosen as the pivot.
terms.connects: Determines what arrangement causes worst-case splits.

terms.meaning: A split where one partition has 0 elements and the other has n-1 elements.
terms.example: Pivot is already the largest: [1,2,3,4,5] with pivot=5.
terms.connects: Leads to O(n^2) total comparisons instead of O(n log n).

terms.meaning: All elements are unique integers in the range 1 to n.
terms.connects: Ensures no duplicates; worst case is purely about ordering.

given.plan: Identify what arrangement makes the last element always the largest (or always the smallest) in every recursive subproblem. A sorted ascending sequence [1,2,...,n] achieves this: each pivot is the largest element in its subarray, causing 0-element and (n-1)-element partitions at every level, giving T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) = O(n^2).

steps.title: Identify worst-case condition
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + Theta(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n)
steps.apply: Worst case occurs when every pivot produces a completely unbalanced partition: one side empty, the other side with n-1 elements. This gives T(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n), which solves to Theta(n^2).
steps.note: Worst case is when pivot is always the minimum or maximum of its subarray.

steps.title: Construct the worst-case input
steps.apply: If the array is already sorted in ascending order [1, 2, 3, ..., n], the last element (n) is the maximum. After partition, the left subarray is [1,2,...,n-1] (sorted) and right is empty. In the recursive call on [1,2,...,n-1], the last element (n-1) is again the maximum. This pattern repeats at every level, always creating 0/(n-1) splits. Similarly, descending order [n, n-1,...,2,1] also creates worst-case behavior since the last element (1) is always the minimum.
steps.note: Both ascending and descending sorted orders are valid worst-case inputs.

solution.result: A sorted array in ascending order (1, 2, 3, ..., n) or descending order (n, n-1, ..., 1) causes maximum time O(n^2) for Quicksort with last-element pivot.

questions.formula_note: Worst-case recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n) for Quicksort with maximally unbalanced partitions, solving to Theta(n^2).

questions.question:
Quick-sort is run on two inputs shown below to sort in ascending order taking first element as pivot:
i.  1, 2, 3, ..., n
ii. n, n-1, n-2, ..., 2, 1
Let C_1 and C_2 be the number of comparisons made for the inputs (i) and (ii) respectively. Then,
A. C_1 < C_2
B. C_1 > C_2
C. C_1 = C_2
D. we cannot say anything for arbitrary n
questions.to_find: Relationship between the number of comparisons made by Quicksort (first-element pivot) on sorted ascending input vs. reverse-sorted input.

understand.plain: Quicksort with first element as pivot on an already sorted array (either ascending or descending) always creates maximally unbalanced partitions: the pivot is always the smallest (for ascending) or the largest (for descending), giving one empty partition and one of size n-1. In both cases the number of comparisons is exactly the same: n-1 at the first level, n-2 at the second, etc., totaling n(n-1)/2.

keywords.explain: The first element of the current subarray is chosen as the pivot for partitioning.
The total count of element comparisons made during the entire execution of Quicksort.
keywords.example: For [1,2,3], pivot=1: 2 comparisons to place 1, then pivot=2: 1 comparison => total 3 = n(n-1)/2 for n=3.

understand.visual_alt: Both sorted (ascending) and reverse-sorted (descending) inputs produce the same recursion tree depth n and same total comparisons n(n-1)/2 when the first element is the pivot, because both are worst cases.

given.aim: Compare C_1 (comparisons on 1,2,...,n) with C_2 (comparisons on n,n-1,...,1) for first-element-pivot Quicksort.

terms.connects: First element (1) is always minimum => empty left partition at each step.
First element (n) is always maximum => empty right partition at each step.

terms.meaning: Total number of element comparisons for each input.
terms.connects: We want to determine the relationship between these two values.

given.plan: Count the comparisons for each input. For input (i), pivot is always the minimum element so left partition is empty, right has n-1 elements. For input (ii), pivot is always the maximum so right partition is empty, left has n-1 elements (after placing pivot). In both cases the recurrence is T(n) = T(n-1) + (n-1), giving total comparisons = sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k = n(n-1)/2.

steps.title: Analyze input (i): 1, 2, ..., n with first-element pivot
steps.apply: Pivot = 1 (minimum). After partitioning, left = [] (empty), right = [2,3,...,n]. Then pivot = 2, left = [] (empty), right = [3,...,n]. Continuing, at each step i the subarray has size n-i+1 and makes n-i comparisons. Total: C_1 = (n-1)+(n-2)+...+1 = n(n-1)/2.
steps.note: This is the classic worst case for first-element pivot on sorted ascending input.

steps.title: Analyze input (ii): n, n-1, ..., 1 with first-element pivot
steps.apply: Pivot = n (maximum). After partitioning, left = [n-1,n-2,...,1] (size n-1), right = [] (empty). Pivot in left subarray = n-1 (maximum of that subarray), left = [n-2,...,1], right = []. At each step i the subarray has size n-i+1 and makes n-i comparisons. Total: C_2 = (n-1)+(n-2)+...+1 = n(n-1)/2.
steps.note: Same recurrence structure as input (i), yielding identical total comparison count.

steps.title: Compare C_1 and C_2
steps.apply: Both inputs are worst-case scenarios for first-element-pivot Quicksort, producing the same total number of comparisons n(n-1)/2.
steps.note: Both ascending-sorted and descending-sorted arrays are equivalent worst cases.

solution.result: C_1 = C_2, so the answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Worst-case Quicksort comparison count: n(n-1)/2, achieved for both sorted and reverse-sorted inputs with first-element pivot.

questions.question:
Randomized quicksort is an extension of quicksort where the pivot is chosen randomly. What is the worst case complexity of sorting n numbers using Randomized quicksort?
A. O(n)
B. O(n log n)
C. O(n^2)
D. O(n!)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexity of Randomized Quicksort.

understand.plain: Randomized Quicksort picks the pivot randomly to avoid the worst case for adversarial inputs. However, in the absolute worst case (an extremely unlucky sequence of random choices), the pivot can always turn out to be the minimum or maximum element, leading to maximally unbalanced partitions just like deterministic worst-case Quicksort. The probability of this happening is astronomically small, but it is still possible, so the worst-case complexity remains O(n^2).

keywords.explain: A variant of Quicksort that chooses the pivot uniformly at random from the current subarray.
keywords.example: For [5,3,1,4,2], instead of always picking 5 or 2, it picks any of the 5 elements randomly.

keywords.explain: The time complexity for the most unfavorable execution (not the expected/average case).
keywords.example: Even with random pivot, if all random choices happen to select the minimum each time, we get O(n^2).

understand.visual_alt: Randomized Quicksort: expected case is O(n log n) but in the absolute worst case (all pivots are minimum/maximum due to unlucky random choices) it degrades to O(n^2).

given.aim: Determine the worst-case time complexity of Randomized Quicksort.

terms.meaning: At each recursive call, a random element is chosen as the pivot.
terms.example: For subarray of size 5, any of the 5 elements is chosen with equal probability 1/5.
terms.connects: Eliminates adversarial inputs but does not eliminate all bad random outcomes.

terms.meaning: The average-case complexity over all random pivot choices is O(n log n).
terms.example: On average, random pivot splits array roughly in half at each level.
terms.connects: This is the practical reason for using randomization, but worst case is still possible.

terms.meaning: The absolute worst sequence of random pivot choices (e.g., always picking the minimum).
terms.example: If all n pivots are minimums: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) => O(n^2).
terms.connects: Though improbable, this theoretical worst case remains O(n^2).

given.plan: Randomization ensures no fixed input is always worst-case, but does not eliminate the theoretical worst-case possibility. If all random choices happen to be the minimum or maximum element, the same O(n^2) recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) applies.

steps.title: Recall worst-case Quicksort recurrence
steps.apply: Quicksort's worst case occurs when every partition is maximally unbalanced (pivot is always the minimum or maximum of the subarray). This gives T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + Theta(n) = T(n-1) + Theta(n), which resolves to Theta(n^2).
steps.note: This is independent of how the pivot is chosen — what matters is what element is selected.

steps.title: Apply to Randomized Quicksort
steps.apply: In Randomized Quicksort, the pivot is chosen randomly. In expectation, random pivot gives O(n log n) time. However, the worst case is when all n random choices happen to select the minimum (or maximum) element at each level. This is an event with probability (1/n)! but it is not impossible. Therefore, the worst-case complexity is still O(n^2).
steps.note: Randomization converts a deterministic worst case to an improbable one, but cannot guarantee O(n log n) worst case.

solution.result: The worst-case complexity of Randomized Quicksort is O(n^2). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Randomized Quicksort: expected time O(n log n), worst-case time O(n^2) (though the worst case is now input-independent and happens only with negligible probability).

questions.question:
The median of n elements can be found in O(n) time. Which one of the following is correct about the complexity of quick sort, in which median is selected as pivot?
A. Theta(n)
B. O(n log n)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. Theta(n^2)
questions.to_find: Time complexity of Quicksort when the median is always chosen as the pivot (with median found in O(n) time).

understand.plain: If we always pick the median as the pivot, every partition is perfectly balanced: each subarray is exactly half the size of the current array. This is the best possible split for Quicksort, giving a recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n). By the Master Theorem, this solves to Theta(n log n). Since using the median guarantees this for every input, the complexity is Theta(n log n) (not just O(n log n)).

keywords.explain: Choosing the median of the current subarray as the pivot ensures a perfectly balanced partition into two equal halves.
keywords.example: Median of [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6] is around 3.5, so about half elements go left and half right.

keywords.explain: A method to solve recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n).
keywords.example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) => T(n) = Theta(n log n).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='140' viewBox='0 0 400 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='150' y='10' width='100' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n</text><line x1='140' y1='40' x2='80' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='40' x2='320' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='70' width='80' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='89' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n/2</text><rect x='280' y='70' width='80' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='89' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n/2</text><text x='200' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) => Theta(n log n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree for median-pivot Quicksort: root splits into two equal halves of size n/2, each level does O(n) work, log n levels total, giving Theta(n log n).

given.aim: Find the complexity of Quicksort when median is always used as pivot (median found in O(n) time).

terms.meaning: Using the linear-time median-of-medians algorithm, the exact median can be computed in O(n) time.
terms.example: For [3,1,4,1,5], median = 3, found in O(5) time.
terms.connects: The O(n) median-finding cost is the 'f(n)' in the Quicksort recurrence.

terms.meaning: Median pivot guarantees both partitions have exactly (n-1)/2 elements.
terms.example: n=7: both subarrays have 3 elements each.
terms.connects: Balanced splits give the best possible recursion depth of log_2(n).

terms.meaning: Two recursive calls each of size n/2, plus O(n) work for median-finding and partitioning.
terms.connects: Solved by Master Theorem to Theta(n log n).

given.plan: Write the recurrence for median-pivot Quicksort and solve using the Master Theorem.

steps.title: Write the recurrence
steps.apply: With median as pivot: partition is perfectly balanced into two halves of size n/2 each. Finding the median costs O(n), and partitioning costs O(n). So T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n).
steps.note: Both median-finding and partitioning contribute O(n) to the non-recursive cost, but their sum is still O(n).

steps.title: Apply Master Theorem
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): a=2, b=2, f(n)=O(n), n^(log_b a) = n^1 = n => Case 2
steps.apply: a=2, b=2, f(n)=Theta(n), n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n = Theta(n). Since f(n) = Theta(n^(log_b a)), this is Master Theorem Case 2: T(n) = Theta(n log n).
steps.note: Since median as pivot is always guaranteed (not just average case), the complexity is Theta(n log n), not just O(n log n).

solution.result: Quicksort with median as pivot runs in Theta(n log n) time. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Master Theorem Case 2: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Theta(n) => T(n) = Theta(n log n). Median-of-medians guarantees optimal Quicksort partition.

questions.question:
Consider the Quicksort algorithm. Suppose there is a procedure for finding a pivot element which splits the list into two sub-lists each of which contains at least one-fifth of the elements. Let T(n) be the number of comparisons required to sort n elements. Then
A. T(n) <= 2T(n/5) + n
B. T(n) <= T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n
C. T(n) <= 2T(4n/5) + n
D. T(n) <= 2T(n/2) + n
questions.to_find: The correct recurrence relation for Quicksort when the pivot guarantees each sublist has at least n/5 elements.

understand.plain: The pivot splits the array such that each part has at least 1/5 of the elements. The worst case for such a pivot is when one part has exactly 1/5 of the elements and the other has 4/5 (since if one side is at least 1/5, the other is at most 4/5). The recurrence reflects this worst-case split: T(n/5) for the smaller subproblem, T(4n/5) for the larger, plus n comparisons for partitioning.

keywords.explain: A pivot that ensures neither partition is smaller than n/5 elements.
keywords.example: For n=10, both partitions have at least 2 elements each.

keywords.explain: Given the guarantee of at least n/5, the worst case is exactly n/5 on one side and 4n/5 on the other.
keywords.example: For n=10: best case 5|5, worst case 2|8 (2 = 10/5).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='130' viewBox='0 0 400 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='150' y='10' width='100' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n</text><line x1='170' y1='40' x2='80' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='230' y1='40' x2='310' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='75' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='75' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n/5 (min)</text><rect x='260' y='75' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='305' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4n/5 (max)</text><text x='200' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Worst split: T(n) <= T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Worst-case split when pivot guarantees at least n/5 per side: one subarray of size n/5 and another of size 4n/5, with n comparison work at the current level.

given.aim: Write a recurrence for Quicksort where the pivot guarantees each sublist has at least n/5 elements.

terms.meaning: Each of the two partitions after pivot placement has at least n/5 elements.
terms.example: n=10: each partition has at least 2 elements.
terms.connects: The worst case within this guarantee is a n/5 vs 4n/5 split.

terms.meaning: Recursion on the smaller and larger parts of the worst-case split.
terms.connects: The sum of subproblem sizes is n-1 (excluding pivot), approximately n.

terms.meaning: Each partitioning step scans all n elements, costing O(n) = n comparisons.
terms.connects: This is the +n term in the recurrence.

given.plan: The pivot guarantee means worst case is n/5 vs 4n/5 split (not 0 vs n-1). Write the recurrence as T(n) <= T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n, corresponding to the worst valid split given the guarantee.

steps.title: Identify worst-case split within the guarantee
steps.apply: The pivot guarantees each partition has at least n/5 elements. Since both parts together sum to n-1 (n minus the pivot), the worst case is the most unbalanced allowed: one part has exactly n/5 and the other has 4n/5 (approximately, since n/5 + 4n/5 = n).
steps.note: Smaller partition = n/5, larger = 4n/5; this is the most unbalanced split the guarantee allows.

steps.title: Write the recurrence
steps.apply: Two recursive calls: T(n/5) for the smaller part and T(4n/5) for the larger part, plus O(n) = n comparisons for the partition step. The recurrence is T(n) <= T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n.
steps.note: This is the correct upper bound. Using T(4n/5) in both calls (option C) would be overly pessimistic since the other part must be at most 4n/5.

steps.title: Solve the recurrence (verification)
steps.apply: T(n) = T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n. Using the Akra-Bazzi method or recursion tree: at each level the total work is n (since n/5 + 4n/5 = n), and the depth is log_{5/4}(n) (the larger subproblem shrinks by factor 4/5 each time). Total work = n * log_{5/4}(n) = O(n log n). This confirms the answer is Theta(n log n).
steps.note: The recursion tree has O(log n) depth and O(n) work per level.

solution.result: T(n) <= T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Unbalanced-partition Quicksort recurrence T(n) = T(n/5) + T(4n/5) + n, solved to Theta(n log n) by recursion tree or Akra-Bazzi method.

questions.question:
In quick-sort, for sorting n elements, the (n/4)th smallest element is selected as pivot using an O(n) time algorithm. What is the worst case time complexity of the quick sort?
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n log n)
C. Theta(n^2)
D. Theta(n^2 log n)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexity of Quicksort when the (n/4)th smallest element is always chosen as pivot.

understand.plain: When the (n/4)th smallest element is used as pivot, the partition always creates two subarrays: one with (n/4 - 1) elements (those smaller than the pivot) and one with (3n/4) elements (those larger). This is a fixed (n/4):(3n/4) split at every level — not a worst-case split like 1:(n-1). With constant-fraction partitions and O(n) pivot-finding cost, the Quicksort recurrence becomes T(n) = T(n/4) + T(3n/4) + O(n), which solves to Theta(n log n).

keywords.explain: Always choosing the element ranked n/4 from the bottom guarantees a fixed n/4 vs 3n/4 split.
keywords.example: n=8: 2nd smallest is pivot; left has 1 element, right has 6 elements.

keywords.explain: A partition where both sides are a constant fraction of n (not 0 and n-1). Any split of the form (cn) and ((1-c)n) for constant 0 < c < 1 gives O(n log n) recursion.
keywords.example: 1/4 vs 3/4 split: both fractions are positive constants less than 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='130' viewBox='0 0 400 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='150' y='10' width='100' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n</text><line x1='170' y1='40' x2='80' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='230' y1='40' x2='310' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='75' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='75' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n/4</text><rect x='265' y='75' width='90' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3n/4</text><text x='200' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T(n) = T(n/4) + T(3n/4) + O(n) => Theta(n log n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree for Quicksort with (n/4)th smallest as pivot: fixed n/4 vs 3n/4 split at every level, O(n) work per level, depth O(log n), total Theta(n log n).

given.aim: Determine the worst-case complexity of Quicksort when (n/4)th smallest is always chosen as pivot in O(n) time.

terms.meaning: The element that, when the array is sorted, falls at position n/4 (25th percentile).
terms.example: In a 12-element array, 3rd smallest is the pivot.
terms.connects: Choosing this always creates a n/4 vs 3n/4 partition.

terms.meaning: The pivot is found in linear time (e.g., using median-of-medians or a selection algorithm).
terms.example: Finding the kth smallest in O(n) time using the BFPRT algorithm.
terms.connects: This contributes the O(n) non-recursive cost to the recurrence.

terms.meaning: Two recursive calls with sizes n/4 and 3n/4, plus O(n) for pivot-finding and partitioning.
terms.connects: This is solved using the recursion tree method.

given.plan: Write the recurrence for (n/4)th smallest pivot Quicksort, then solve using the recursion tree method by analyzing total work per level.

steps.title: Determine the partition sizes
steps.apply: The (n/4)th smallest element divides the array into: left subarray with n/4 - 1 (approximately n/4) elements smaller than the pivot, and right subarray with 3n/4 elements larger than the pivot. So every partition creates subarrays of size n/4 and 3n/4.
steps.note: This is a fixed constant-fraction split, not a worst-case 0:(n-1) split.

steps.title: Write and analyze the recurrence
steps.apply: T(n) = T(n/4) + T(3n/4) + O(n). Using the recursion tree: at level 0, work = n. At level 1, two subproblems of sizes n/4 and 3n/4, total work = n/4 + 3n/4 = n. At every level, total work = n. The tree depth is determined by the larger subproblem: 3n/4 shrinks to 1 in log_{4/3}(n) levels. So total levels = log_{4/3}(n) = O(log n). Total work = n * O(log n) = Theta(n log n).
steps.note: Any constant fraction split (c*n and (1-c)*n for 0 < c < 1) gives Theta(n log n) by this argument.

steps.title: Confirm this is the worst case
steps.apply: Since the pivot selection algorithm always finds exactly the (n/4)th smallest element, the partition is always exactly n/4 vs 3n/4. This is deterministic — there is no 'better' or 'worse' input; every input gives the same partition behavior. Therefore, both best and worst case are Theta(n log n).
steps.note: This is different from random pivot where the split varies; here it is fixed at n/4 vs 3n/4 every time.

solution.result: Worst-case time complexity is Theta(n log n). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Constant-fraction split recurrence T(n) = T(n/4) + T(3n/4) + O(n) solves to Theta(n log n) via recursion tree; total work = n per level over O(log n) levels.
